attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the labour ward at 39 weeks gestation with regular contractions every five minutes. This is her first pregnancy, which has been uncomplicated. She has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications. Her antenatal care has been unremarkable with all screening tests within normal limits.

On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 80/min, blood pressure 120/75 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. Abdominal examination reveals a longitudinal lie with cephalic presentation. Vaginal examination shows the cervix is 4 cm dilated with clear liquor. The fetal heart rate is 140 beats per minute on auscultation.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate method of fetal monitoring for this woman?
attributes.explanation:
In this scenario, we have a low-risk woman in active labour at term with no complications. The most appropriate method of fetal monitoring for this patient is intermittent auscultation. This involves listening to the fetal heart rate at regular intervals, typically every 15-30 minutes in the first stage of labour and after each contraction in the second stage.

Continuous cardiotocography (CTG) is not routinely recommended for low-risk pregnancies. While it provides a continuous record of the fetal heart rate and uterine contractions, its use in low-risk labours has been associated with increased rates of caesarean sections and instrumental vaginal births without improving perinatal outcomes. It is generally reserved for high-risk pregnancies or when complications arise during labour.

Intermittent cardiotocography is not a standard practice. If there are concerns that warrant the use of CTG, it should be continuous rather than intermittent.

Continuous fetal scalp electrode monitoring is an invasive form of fetal monitoring that involves attaching an electrode directly to the fetal scalp. It is typically used when external monitoring is inadequate or when there are specific concerns about fetal wellbeing. It is not indicated in this low-risk scenario.

Suggesting no fetal monitoring would be inappropriate, as all labouring women should have some form of fetal monitoring to ensure fetal wellbeing during labour.

It's important to note that if risk factors develop during labour (such as maternal fever, abnormal vaginal bleeding, thick meconium-stained liquor, or abnormalities detected on intermittent auscultation), the method of fetal monitoring should be reassessed. In such cases, a switch to continuous CTG might be warranted. The key is to balance the need for fetal surveillance with the goal of minimising unnecessary interventions in low-risk labours.
learningPoints: Intermittent auscultation is the recommended fetal monitoring method for low-risk women in labour.
Continuous cardiotocography (CTG) is not routinely recommended for low-risk pregnancies due to increased intervention rates without improved outcomes.
Risk factors that may indicate the need for continuous CTG include maternal fever, abnormal vaginal bleeding, and thick meconium-stained liquor.
references: Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists. Intrapartum Fetal Surveillance Clinical Guideline [Internet]. 2021 [cited 2024 Oct 9]. Available from: https://ranzcog.edu.au/wp-content/uploads/2022/05/Intrapartum-Fetal-Surveillance.pdf
